author: @RWBHere
text: Small correction, Robert. Snuffing is not the same as extinguishing. When you snuff a candle, or a lamp,  you are trimming its wick, both to regulate the flame size and to stop it from smoking excessively. Modern candles and most modern lamps or paraffin heaters, don't need snuffing. If you use the correct Carbon wick in that heater, it will never need snuffing, because the Carbon withstands the flame heat without burning back irregularly.

author: @kenjackson5938
text:
Sorry, have to correct something you said. Do not put a CO alarm in a furnace room. 
For starters, CO is almost (high 90s%) the same density of Air. CO mixes readily with air. It does NOT rise to the top like smoke, or sink to the floor like gas or other fuel vapors. That is why Many CO alarms are designed to be a wall plug in. Wall receptacles are typically never near the ceiling. The only reason you have combination Smoke and CO alarms is the convenience of not having to have 2 separate alarms. It has nothing to do with the possibility of the CO rising to the ceiling.
That being said, the reason it is not recommended to put a CO alarm in the furnace room is, fuel burning appliances that exhaust through a roof flue are NOT air tight. On windy days, it is possible to get SMALL amount of exhaust gasses pushed back into the furnace room from the burner box. Go down and listen to your furnace on windy days. Sometimes you can hear the changes in flow of the exhaust. But then the small amount of pushed back gasses will then be mixed with the combustion air and go back up the flue. This can cause a false positive environment for the CO alarm.
The biggest  reason is, very few people will have more than one smoke and CO alarm in their home. You want them placed in an area where you are going to hear them, especially at night when you are sleeping. Next to the bedrooms. No point in having the only CO alarm in the basement, if the bedrooms are up stairs. It wont wake you in the middle of the night down there,, or chances are....

author: @QUADBOYification
text: Its a basic Kerosine heater design with the same principal of a fluid sucking wick with a mesh around the flame. The thinner the mesh the better. Very cheap to build design you made. Ofcourse a Kerosine heater uses pressure to push Fluid through a little generator to pre-heat the Kerosine making it a evaporate into gas and burn more violently. Hence your burning time is reduced but the heat generated is more intense. Makes sense. Your system if carefully re-designed could be used in a tent and even heat a pot of water.  Simply foldable (nested) into each other packed in a sturdy cylinder. The mesh is the expensive part and should be easily replaceable on the go. To glow the whole mesh (ideal) the wick should be used with a special heater head. Something that burns the full length of the mesh. Probably a tube with very tiny holes over its entire area.  Genious, thanks.

author: @MiguelFormigo
text:
I found this videos quite interesting, and came to it in sequence from the previous 1702 DIY Heater building video.
However, a worrisome though came up regarding all those different fuels options and I did a little searching regarding some of them.

i.e.:
methanol has adverse effects in contact with skin and eyes, and even when inhaled in its liquid form, and when burned it releases, and I quote, "irritating, corrosive, and/or toxic gases" that "may travel to the source of ignition and flash back". I also read that those gases are considered "negligible emissions" and it is safe to use indoors, however I find this information a bit contradictory.

bioethanol, on the other hand, seems like a safer option as, and I quote, "do not require a venting system with a chimney or flue as they do not emit harmful emissions".

regarding isopropyl alcohol, again I found mixed contradictory articles, some saying it is safe for indoor use and even cooking, other claiming it may pose a health risk as it "can irritate the nose and throat" and "repeated high exposure can cause headache, dizziness, confusion, loss of coordination, unconsciousness and even death".

of course that with any of these fuel options there is always the DANGEROUS FIRE HAZARD component to take into account, for the sheer nature of the chemical themselves, so this is always something to have in mind. but regarding their use as a heat source and consequent fumes they may release, information is somewhat contradictory and a bit confusing.
could you maybe clear this issue?

author: @elgorrion52
text:
A great video as ever! 
Regarding ventilation, as a comparison Natural Gas fires fitted to a chimney only need a vent providing outside air for combustion for 7kw input or greater, the average gas fire being only 5kw, "adventitious ventilation" - draughts under doors etc - being enough. Decorative flame effect fires DO need a vent: (yellow flames = incomplete combustion). A chimney/flue is still needed for the above.

I couldn't begin to calculate the input of one of these heaters, but I would favour using only the fuels that burn blue (yellow flames = incomplete combustion, soot) - then you could warm tortillas on the top bit! 
The fact that it's a radiant heater means you can "heat the person not the space" so you can feel the benefit quickly without depleting the oxygen in the room, but use caution with any open flame appliance, maybe get a carbon monoxide alarm, check for soot etc. Room size matters.
If this sounds a bit r-c, a friend of mine died of CO poisonning by falling asleep in his campervan with the cooker on to keep warm.

author: @justinw1765
text: The easiest way to vent these stoves is to create an insert for a window and put a chimney through it. For little stoves like this, a 2 to 3" or so chimney should work ok. To save money/cost, use steel for like the first foot or so, but then use Al for the rest of the pipe. Make sure you paint the chimney pipe with some kind of high temp paint to increase the emissivity (so that the IR radiates out of the pipe better).

text: I add a bit of epsom salt, copper sulfate , borax or table salt to make the flame a little more visible.. regular table salt will change the flames to a yellow, copper sulfate=green, borax=light green, epsom salt= orange, you can also add a copper wire at the wick top and get a blue/green flame, salt substitute/potassium chloride gives a purpleish color

author: @KeithOlson
text:
I just had a few thoughts: 

1. The flames travel up the outside of the mesh.  What about using multiple layers of mesh with spaces in between them?  That will multiply the surface area that can be heated up and radiate IR.
2. Instead of a lid, what about using cheap stainless steel scrubbers, stacked inside the mantle?  That would also increase the mass while still allowing air flow.
3. Depending on the stainless steel scrubbers available to you, can you check to see if they can be unrolled to be used as an easily-available mantle?  If not, could wire be used to hold them in place so they act as a mantle while in their original form?

Cheers!

replies.author: @regwatson2017
replies.text: Number 1 suggestion sounds great for Rob to check out - Will it use more oil and be hotter, use the same, or will the heat output be comparatively reduced because of the greater heating surface area required ?

replies.author: @ScottyHugefellow
replies.text: @regwatson2017  the majority of the heat will escape either way so a larger surface area would catch more that would otherwise be wasted. Pretty sure steel wool gets weird when its been heated up, im not sure what a steel scrubber is but it seems like steel wool

replies.author: @denisripley8699
replies.text: @ScottyHugefellow Steel wool made with tiny filaments of steel (which readily oxidizes); Stainless Steel 'scrubber' filaments very much larger -----> greater thermal mass, so less likely to become red hot/vapourise.  Copper scrubbers even better conduction.

replies.author: @ScottyHugefellow
@denisripley8699
replies.text: @ScottyHugefellow Sorry Scotty.. I'm in my angloshere bubble !  A scrubber or scourer is, like you suggest, a heavy duty, durable version of steel wool, almost always made of stainless steel and unlikely to irritate bare fingers like steel wool does. It's the stuff that's also used in homemade distillation columns, although copper 'scrubbers' or mesh is a better option in that application.

text: Is ethanol  the same as meths

replies.author: @DFPercush
replies.text: meth- prefix means one carbon atom. eth- prefix means two carbon atoms. "-ane" means a hydrocarbon, like natural gas or oil. "-ol" means an alchohol, where one of the hydrogens in the "ane" is replaced by an -OH.  Methanol and Ethanol are very different when it comes to toxicity, you don't want to drink methanol, but they burn about the same. Ethanol has a slightly higher energy density.

text: I tested a version using Glass Fibre (loft insulation) and Ceramic Fibre. Maybe they would make good wick alternatives?

replies.author: @TheOtherBrook
replies.text: I tested ceramic fiber and ceramic paper with waste oil and vegetable oil. Both wicked and burned it without real degradation. The ceramic paper was a bit soft, it would fall apart on its own if left only as a roll, but seemed OK rolled and stuffed in a metal tube with about 1/4" showing. You did need to be careful of it though to make sure you didn't brush off the part sticking out. The ceramic fiber is more like the batting of the glass fiber, a big wad. You have to be careful of it though because small bits can break off and waft, leading to possible respiratory irritation. I put some in about a 2" tin with a lip over the mat and added some stainless screen (#20 I think) over the top to try to make sure none broke free. Worked well as an isopropyl burner, as well as with the fuels mentioned above. Of the three, I like the carbon felt though just because it seems to be the best at keeping itself together without worrying about parts breaking off or blowing away. I don't think the flames got hot enough to need the about 400 deg. F extra of the ceramics. Incidentally I also tried the glass fiber from insulation and the ceramic fiber, with a tighter weave, seemed to do better with the isopropyl and thinner fuels.

replies.text: @Milkybar3320011 carbon monoxide when it burns it only leaves behind co2 and water vapor acetylene lamps have been used for almost 200 years safely

replies.author: @DFPercush
replies.text:
Carbon monoxide is a majority of wood gas, if you ever see people with wood gasifiers that's mostly what comes out. It does burn, but it has a hard time sustaining its own flame, so you need to make sure there's a pilot flame or something else in the mix. Obviously not for indoor use.
Acetylene is a neat chemical, that's what old miner's head lamps and even headlights in early cars and motorcycles used. I got to take a close up look at an old Scott motorcycle from 1929 that used such a system. You load some calcium carbide in the chamber and turn a knob to adjust a water dripper, then the gas goes up a tube to the head lamp, you pull out a match and light it up. I have a feeling calcium carbide is more expensive than alcohol though.

author: @Liowen
text: I have been curious if you could add carbon felt to a round kerosene heater?  Now I know the greenhouse heater didn't work with the carbon felt, however I wonder if it would have worked if a piece of copper tubing was used to hold the wick under the heating element.  If so then the same idea could be used on a round kerosene heater and be a long term heating solution if the need arises, and as a bonus you could use other fuels if need be instead of just kerosene.  Also the round kerosene heaters are kind of a mantle based heater just scaled up to 11 so it does kind of fit in with this idea.

replies.author: @pattiannepascual
replies.text: yes. I have several gallons of used vegetable oil but have yet to create a heater that isn't a giant mess to maintain.i got heat but what a disaster. I put little holes in the metal caps of beer bottles I found all over the neighborhood. put cotton string in each,1/2 filled an 8x8 baking pan with used oil and put 9 caps with socks. it created a ton of heat but when you had to adjust the wicks, like I made them too high and they were smoking, it meant lifting the cap and getting oil all over. I saw a few on YouTube that made cans with holes for the rocks, etc, but all had the same issue. if only they could make an adjustable wick holder like kerosene lamps have, which don't work with oil because it's too heavy to travel up the wick and the space in between too far from the oil.I did experiment, including using the kerosene lamp top and keeping it at or under oil level but my experiments failed.

author: @Chimel31
text:
"Use the same precautions you would use around open flames" does not mean much to viewers who find your experiments very interesting and pedagogical but who have no or limited experience with open flames. That's why I would still recommend to translate this sentence into an actual combustion chamber with external air intake and exhaust and zero contact between the air in the combustion chamber and the air in the room.
It's not that easy for a regular viewer to design such a system, especially as a cheap DIY, while it would make an excellent video on its own and a safe burner for any further experiments. 2 poor little birds with one stone.

replies.author: @401ksolar

author: @BH-hr9tp
text: I grew sunflowers collected the seeds and processed them into oil, I only got 1 kilo which gave 0.5 litres of oil. Scaled up it would be worth it but you need a pretty big patch of land to get a decent amount and you could turn it into biodiesel, but the oil on its own would still burn.

replies.author: @islandsedition
replies.text:
that seems like a good yield for the weight. presumably the much of the left over matter is protein? 

how much area did you need to get the kilo of seeds?

replies.author: @BH-hr9tp
replies.text:
I did 50 plants in an area 1m by 4 m, 8 plants died , some seeds were raided by birds , position wasn’t ideal as they didn’t get sun till midday and I didn’t feed them. The seeds were from a pet shop so I don’t know what variety they were , but it said oil seed for birds on the packet
I did try to get some Petrovak seeds , that’s what the Russians and Ukrainians grow but couldn’t get hold of any. A good experiment though .if you can grow a bulk lot they store for years. Going to try rapeseed next year as well.

replies.author: @islandsedition

replies.author: @401ksolar
replies.text: I have what I think is a translation question, where I am from paraffin is a hard wax it is used to make candles it is also used in Old School canning methods like jellies and jam, are we talking the same substance or is paraffin in liquid form on the other side of the earth?

replies.author: @l0I0I0I0
replies.text: Paraffins have amazing heat capacity without expansion.  At the same time, it can be explosive under the right conditions.  It's cheap also.  Might want to use paraffin for heat storage, as long as it stays at the lower stages of liquid, as it expands crazy explosive like at the gas stage.  I think it will be an amazing option for heat storage.  GL!

replies.author: @401ksolar
replies.text: @l0I0I0I0 okay, so I take it by referring to paraffin's in the plural we must be talking about different viscosity materials, is that a correct assessment?

replies.author: @l0I0I0I0
replies.text: @401ksolar I know different paraffins have different melting points and different purities but have not looked into the exact differences as I decided to use something else for specialized application.  However like most substances, it storage most of the heat, or releases it at the entrapies transitions between solid to liquid, liquid to gas, etc.  If you want to use it build around the melting temp as it can store the most energy, or release it, at the transitions just like most materials.
